Home»Lifestyle»Paris mayor Anne Hidalgo swims in the Seine ahead of Olympics

Paris Mayor Anne Hidalgo celebrated a historic milestone by swimming in the Seine, fulfilling a promise to make the river swimmable.

In a momentous occasion, Paris Mayor Anne Hidalgo took a refreshing dip in the Seine on Wednesday, fulfilling a promise decades in the making.

The river, long deemed too polluted for swimming, has undergone a dramatic cleanup just in time for the 2024 Olympics.

The city’s ambitious efforts to make the Seine swimmable have finally paid off. This marks a significant milestone for Paris and the upcomingGames.

Hidalgo led a group of around 100 officials, athletes, and locals in the celebratory swim, according toThe Guardian.

The event was met with cheers and applause as she donned a swimsuit and plunged into the river.

“It’s a dream day … and the sun is out,” Hidalgo exclaimed, completing a 100-metre crawl in the 20C (68F) water.

“It’s sweet and wonderful and the result of a lot of work.”

The journey to this point began in 2015 when the international triathlon federation suggested holding a triathlon in the Seine.

The question loomed: could athletes swim in the Seine?

The answer has been a resounding yes.

Tony Estanguet, president of the Paris 2024 organising committee, praised the city’s efforts.

“Today we have confirmation that the Seine is swimmable and that the triathlon and marathon swimming competitions can take place in it,” he said.

The river’s cleanup has been a monumental task. Swimming in the Seine has been banned since 1923 due to health risks, according toCNN.

In 1990, then-mayor Jacques Chirac vowed to clean the river, but his efforts fell short. Hidalgo’s achievement is a testament to perseverance and innovation.

The extensive operation included constructing a €1.4bn (R 28 378 billion) holding and treatment tank to manage stormwater and improve the wastewater network.

This massive undertaking faced numerous challenges, according toTime Magazine.

The Seine had to pass stringent water quality tests for E. coli bacteria, an indicator of faecal matter.

Only recently did the river meet the necessary standards, eliminating 75% of identified bacterial pollution.

Regular testing will continue at various points to ensure safety during the Olympicevents.

The triathlon events are scheduled for 30, 31 July, and 5 August, with a swimming marathon on 8 and 9 August. The paratriathlon will follow on 1 and 2 September.

Competitors will set off from Pont Alexandre III, a marvel of 19th-century engineering near the Champs-Élysées. The Eiffel Tower will provide a picturesque backdrop.

The cleanup operation was not without its uncertainties. Heavy rains posed a significant risk, potentially spiking water pollution levels.

Contingency plans are in place to postpone races if necessary, ensuring theathletes’ safety.

“As organisers we’re very happy to be able to offer athletes the best conditions,” Estanguet stated.

“But over and above that, what is at play is using the Games to speed up the transformation of the city.”

Hidalgo’s swim, twice postponed due to fluctuating pollution levels, came just 10 days before the Olympics’ opening. The event symbolised hope and progress.

French experts have identified more than 30 species of fish in the Seine in Paris, compared to just three in 1970, highlighting the river’s revival.

The successful cleanup has broader implications. It will help Paris adapt to the climate crisis and mitigate ocean pollution.

After her swim, Hidalgo reflected on the achievement.

“Making the Seine swimmable would also help the city adapt to the climate crisis and mitigate the pollution of the ocean into which the river flows,” she said.

This historic swim marks a new era for Paris. The Seine, once a symbol of pollution, now represents renewal and resilience.

As the city prepares for the 2024 Olympics, the river’s transformation stands as a testament to what determination and innovation can achieve.

The Seine River, flowing through the heart of Paris, has been a vital artery for the city since ancient times.

It has served as a crucial waterway for trade, transport, and commerce, shaping the economic and cultural development of Paris.

The riverbanks have witnessed historical events, from Roman conquests to medieval markets and revolutionary uprisings.

However, industrialisation and urbanisation led to severe pollution, prompting a swimming ban in 1923. What do you think about Hidalgo’s accomplishment?

title: Paris mayor Anne Hidalgo swims in the Seine ahead of Olympics
author: Precious Simpasa
description: Paris Mayor Anne Hidalgo celebrated a historic milestone by swimming in the Seine, fulfilling a promise to make the river swimmable.
